column1:
Amaurosis fugax can be described as:

A: TIA of the eye
B: TIA of the frontal lobe
C: CVA of the eye
D: CVA of the frontal lobe
column2:
Answer: A. TIA of the eye

Feedback:
Because the symptom of amaurosis fugax is usually fleeting, it is termed a transient ischemic attack of the eye.

column1:
When compared to the normal left CCA, the right CCA demonstrates reduced velocity, slower upstroke and holodiastolic flow. These findings are most consistent with:

A: elevated hematocrit levels
B: congestive heart failure
C: stenosis proximal to the point of insonation
D: decreased hematocrit levels
column2:
Answer: C. stenosis proximal to the point of insonation

Feedback:
If a single CCA demonstrates reduced velocity, slower upstroke and holodiastolic flow = stenosis proximal to the point of
insonation. The vessel demonstrates the characteristics of flow distal to a stenosis. The other choices would cause
hemodynamic changes in both carotid arteries.

column1:
Which of the following vessels can provide a collateral pathway to redistribute flow from one side of the brain to the other side?

A: carotid siphon
B: middle cerebral artery
C: posterior communicator artery
D: anterior communicator and basilar artery
column2:
Answer: D. anterior communicator and basilar artery

Feedback:
The anterior communicating arteries can allow flow to move from one ACA into the other. If the left ICA is occluded, flow from
the right ICA enters the brain and travels to the right ACA. Flow then moves through the communicator to fill the left ACA in a
retrograde fashion.
The cerebellum is supplied by blood from branches of the vertebral arteries and basilar artery. If the left vertebral is occluded
proximally, flow moves cephalad toward the brain in the right vertebral artery and then some of this flow is redirected caudally
into the left vertebral (retrograde filling).

column1:
A carotid Doppler exam demonstrates an occlusion of the left ICA, but the distal ICA demonstrates retrograde flow. The ECA demonstrates antegrade flow with increased diastolic flow. The ophthalmic artery on the left side demonstrates flow moving away from the transducer. Where is the collateral flow coming from?

A: ophthalmic to ophthalmic collateralization
B: cross over collateralization through the ACA
C: ECA to ICA collateralization
D: vertebrobasilar collateralization
column2:
Answer: C. ECA to ICA collateralization

Feedback:
Flow reversal in the ophthalmic artery is a key sign of ECA to ICA collateral flow. If the ICA is obstructed proximally, collateral
flow can refill the ICA distally. Flow moves from the left ECA - left superficial temporal artery - left supraorbital artery(reversed) -
left ophthalmic artery(reversed) - distal left ICA (reversed)

column1:
Which statement best describes amaurosis fugax?

A: Bilateral blindness for more than 24hrs
B: Spots flashing in front of eyes
C: Dizziness upon standing
D: Monocular blindness
column2:
Answer: D. Monocular blindness

Feedback:
It occurs when a piece of plaque in the carotid artery breaks off and travels to the retinal artery in the eye. In people with
amaurosis fugax, vision loss continues as long as the blood supply to the retinal artery is blocked. This usually only lasts seconds
but may last several minutes. Some patients describe the loss of vision as a black shade coming down over their eye.

column1:
What is the main risk factor for a stroke?

A: Hyperlipidemia
B: Smoking
C: Systemic HTN
D: Diabetes
column2:
Answer: C. Systemic HTN

Feedback:
Systemic HTN is the main risk factor for stroke

column1:
A critical ICA stenosis will have what effect on the ipsilateral middle cerebral artery flow?

A: decreased diastolic flow
B: no effect
C: increased diastolic flow
D: increased systolic flow
column2:
Answer: A. decreased diastolic flow

Feedback:
A waveform distal to a critical stenosis will demonstrate a blunted monophasic waveform with minimal or absent diastolic flow.

column1:
Which of the following is an expected finding with a complete left subclavian steal?

A: steep acceleration in the left subclavian artery waveform
B: increased resistance in the left axillary artery
C: increased vessel size, flow volume and velocity in the right vertebral artery
D: diastolic flow reversal in the left vertebral artery
column2:
Answer: C. increased vessel size, flow volume and velocity in the right vertebral artery

Feedback:
If the left subclavian artery is obstructed, flow will move from the right vertebral into the basilar artery to make a U-turn into the
left vertebral artery. Flow in the right vertebral artery will increase with increased velocity as it tries to meet the increased
demands of supplying the posterior circulation and now the left arm. This usually causes the artery to dilate. Flow in the left
vertebral artery is reversed and the flow enters the left subclavian artery distal to the obstruction. The flow in arteries of the left
arm will demonstrate reduced resistance and acceleration with a monophasic flow pattern.

column1:
Which of the following is the most accurate way to measure diameter and area stenosis in the carotid arteries?

A: Longitudinal view on ultrasound
B: Angiography
C: Transverse view on ultrasound
D: CT without contrast
column2:
Answer: C. Transverse view on ultrasound

Feedback:
Using US, diameter and area stenosis can be measured on the transverse view. The longitudinal view used in ultrasound is not
as accurate for diameter stenosis as a transverse view because of the possibility of off axis images and plaque distribution in the
artery. The longitudinal view cannot be used to assess area stenosis. Angiography only allows for estimation of diameter
stenosis. Contrast is necessary to visualize blood vessels and assess stenosis on CT exams.

column1:
Treatment for internal carotid artery occlusion includes which of the following?

A: stent placement
B: endarterectomy
C: angioplasty
D: none of the above
column2:
Answer: D. none of the above

Feedback:
Occlusions in the carotid arteries are usually not invasively or surgically treated. There is limited benefit to attempting to
revascularize the vessel and the risk of stroke/death is quite significant. Critical stenosis can be treated, occlusions cannot!!

column1:
The flow demonstrated below the baseline is:

A: caused by incoming collateral flow due to the significant stenosis.
B: caused by turbulent flow at the site of the stenosis.
C: noise caused by patient respiration.
D: an example of cross talk artifact.
column2:
Answer: B. caused by turbulent flow at the site of the stenosis.

Feedback:
Eddy currents or vortices of swirling flow are seen just distal to a stenosis. These swirling currents of blood can be
demonstrated as areas of antegrade and retrograde flow occurring simultaneously on the Doppler tracing.

column1:
A patient's carotid exam demonstrates 50-69% stenosis. What will be the recommended treatment for this patient?

A: 6-month follow up exam
B: angiography
C: endarterectomy
D: stent
column2:
Answer: A. 6-month follow up exam

Feedback:
If the exam indicates a 50-69% stenosis, a 6-month follow up exam will be needed. When the stenosis reaches 70% or greater,
treatment options will be considered.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound. The chart states there is a history of dilated cardiomyopathy and an intra-aortic balloon pump is present. Which of the following correctly describes how to evaluate the patient for carotid stenosis?

A: Waveforms from the common carotid artery will be have two systolic peaks and the first one should be measured to determine the peak velocity
B: Waveforms from the common carotid artery will be have two systolic peaks and the second one should be measured to determine the peak velocity
C: The patient should be rescheduled until after the pump is removed because there is no way to evaluate carotid stenosis while a pump is active
D: The peak systolic velocity is invalid, but the end diastolic velocity should be measured
column2:
Answer: A. Waveforms from the common carotid artery will be have two systolic peaks and the first one should be measured to determine the peak velocity

Feedback:
IABP assisted waveforms have 2 systolic peaks for each pulse. The first peak indicates the left ventricular contraction. The
second peak indicates the balloon inflation. EDV cannot be determined in the patients with an IABP because flow reversal is
commonly seen after the balloon deflation. If possible, turn off the IABP during carotid evaluation. If this is not possible, velocity
from the first peak should be used as the PSV.

column1:
Which of the following signs/symptoms is an expected finding with a carotid sinus massage?

A: dizziness
B: urticaria
C: tachycardia
D: hypertension
column2:
Answer: A. dizziness

Feedback:
The carotid sinus massage is used to evaluate a patient for hypersensitivity of the carotid sinus as the potential cause for the
non-specific symptom of syncope. If there is no stenosis identified in the extracranial arteries, this procedure may be
performed. The patient is placed on a tilt table and an EKG with blood pressure monitor is used to assess the patient during the
procedure. A physician will massage the carotid artery at the level of the cricoid cartilage. An abnormal response will lead to
hypotension (>50mmHg drop), bradycardia and feelings of dizziness or syncope. Urticaria refers to the formation of hives which
is usually related to an allergic reaction.

column1:
If the CCA PSV is 80cm/s and the CCA EDV is 40cm/s, what is the diastolic velocity ratio?

A: 0.5
B: 1.0
C: 1.5
D: 2.1
column2:
Answer: D. 2.1

Feedback:
Diastolic Velocity Ratio = EDV ICA / EDV CCA
83cm/s / 40 cm/s = 2.1

column1:
Why would bilateral brachial blood pressure (BP) assessment assist in the diagnosis of a subclavian steal?

A: The systolic BP will be lower on the affected side
B: The highest brachial pressure is needed to calculate the ABI.
C: The systolic BP will be higher in the affected side
D: To evaluate the potential for future limb amputation.
column2:
Answer: A. The systolic BP will be lower on the affected side

Feedback:
For Example: If the left subclavian artery is occluded proximal to the origin of the left vertebral artery, flow from the right
vertebral artery will fill the left vertebral artery in a retrograde fashion. This blood will then fill the distal subclavian artery and
enter the arm. The reduced flow into the left arm will demonstrate reduced pressure.

column1:
Which of the following would be the most likely cause of a false positive diagnosis of ICA stenosis?

A: decreased cardiac output
B: improper Doppler angle
C: tortuosity
D: CHF
column2:
Answer: C. tortuosity

Feedback:
A curved vessel will cause increased velocities at the "kinked" location(s). This can cause a false positive diagnosis of stenosis.
CHF, decreased cardiac output, or an improper Doppler angle can lead to a false negative for ICA stenosis.

column1:
The Doppler signal indicates:

A: an occlusion distal to the current point of insonation.
B: subclavian steal on the ipsilateral side.
C: decreased viscosity.
D: an occlusion proximal to the current point of insonation.
column2:
Answer: A. an occlusion distal to the current point of insonation.

Feedback:
The Doppler waveform demonstrates a low velocity, blunted waveform with no antegrade flow in diastole. This indicates an
occlusion distal to the current point of insonation. The flow pattern is termed "stump flow".
An occlusion proximally would lead to no flow in the ICA or if collaterals are present, flow would be reversed, low resistance flow
that is biphasic.
Decreased viscosity would result in thinner blood and higher than normal velocities.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid Doppler exam due to left sided amaurosis fugax. These findings are most suggestive of stenosis in:

A: right ICA
B: left vertebral artery
C: left ICA
D: right vertebral artery
column2:
Answer: C. left ICA

Feedback:
Visual symptoms occur on the same side as the stenosis because the ophthalmic artery is a branch of the distal ICA.

column1:
Which of the following is true regarding the difference between fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) and atherosclerosis of the internal carotid artery?

A: Atherosclerosis forms within the intima of the vessel while FMD is caused by over-proliferation of the adventitia.
B: Atheroma formation usually occurs in the proximal segment while FMD usually affects the more distal segments of the ICA.
C: Atherosclerosis forms within the adventitia of the vessel while FMD is caused by over-proliferation of the intima.
D: FMD usually leads to aneurysm formation while atherosclerosis leads to stenosis.
column2:
Answer: B. Atheroma formation usually occurs in the proximal segment while FMD usually affects the more distal segments of the ICA.

Feedback:
FMD is related to collagen growth in the media layer of the vessel. Atheroma formation involves the media and intimal layer.
Atheroma formation usually occurs in the proximal segment while FMD usually affects the more distal segments of the ICA.

column1:
What is the expected waveform characteristic if the Doppler sample volume is placed in the mid ICA?

A: peaked waveform with decreased peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
B: late peaking, low velocity, low resistance waveform
C: increased peak systolic velocity and decreased end diastolic velocity
D: biphasic flow with increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
column2:
Answer: B. late peaking, low velocity, low resistance waveform

Feedback:
The calculated diameter stenosis is 76% which indicates a severe stenosis. The waveform at the stenosis will indicate a
monophasic waveform with increased systolic and diastolic flow pattern. A sample taken distal to the stenosis in the mid ICA will
demonstrate a blunted late peaking, low velocity, low resistance waveform.

column1:
The transverse image of the left neck is from a carotid Doppler evaluation. What should you do next for this patient?

A: ask the patient to perform the Valsalva maneuver
B: evaluate the right subclavian vein
C: attempt compression views of the neck
D: evaluate the left subclavian vein
column2:
Answer: D. evaluate the left subclavian vein

Feedback:
The subclavian vein should be evaluated for thrombus propagation. Because the thrombus is very visible within the jugular vein,
compression views are unnecessary and can lead to an increased risk of embolization.

column1:
Bilateral homonymous hemianopia is most commonly seen with:

A: abdominal aortic aneurysm
B: internal carotid artery stenosis
C: vertebrobasilar disease
D: subclavian vein thrombosis
column2:
Answer: C. vertebrobasilar disease

Feedback:
Bilateral visual symptoms are most commonly related to vertebrobasilar disease and reduced flow to the occipital lobes of the
brain. Unilateral visual symptoms are most commonly related to ICA disease and decreased flow to the ophthalmic artery.

column1:
Which of the following symptoms would be most suggestive of significant disease in the middle cerebral artery(MCA)?

A: Contralateral leg paresis
B: Ipsilateral leg paresis
C: Ipsilateral face and arm paresis
D: Contralateral face and arm paresis
column2:
Answer: D. Contralateral face and arm paresis

Feedback:
The MCA feeds the portions of the brain responsible for the face and arm motion. The right side of the brain controls the motor
functions of the contralateral (left) side of the body and vice versa. MCA disease will cause symptoms in the contralateral arms
and face. The ACA is responsible for supplying the brain tissue that controls the motor functions of the legs.

column1:
Which of the following would not result in a carotid bruit?

A: 60% ECA stenosis
B: 75% ICA stenosis
C: 99% ICA stenosis
D: aortic valve stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. 99% ICA stenosis

Feedback:
99% stenosis will have minimal antegrade flow (string sign) that will not cause an audible bruit. Absence of a bruit does not rule
out a critical stenosis. A CCA occlusion will not cause a bruit but an ICA occlusion may demonstrate a bruit. The diversion of all
ICA flow into the ECA can cause turbulence and increased velocities.

column1:
This color Doppler image demonstrates:

A: normal bilateral vertebral flow
B: reversed right vertebral artery flow
C: subclavian steal
D: left vertebral stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. subclavian steal

Feedback:
When evaluating the vertebral arteries, always take note of the color map and related direction of flow. Note the right vertebral
is flowing in the normal direction toward the brain while the left vertebral demonstrates flow reversal.

column1:
Which of the following describes the appearance of a waveform distal to a critical carotid stenosis?

A: decreased diastolic flow
B: increased systolic flow
C: large spectral window
D: increased diastolic flow
column2:
Answer: A. decreased diastolic flow

Feedback:
The waveform of the flow distal to a critical ICA stenosis will be blunted, low systolic and diastolic peaks and turbulent flow with
loss of the spectral window.

column1:
A patient presents for Carotid Doppler exam following a recent MVA. What is demonstrated on the images provided from this patient's exam?

A: Normal images of CCA flow
B: Pseudoaneurysm of the CCA
C: Improper color Doppler scale settings
D: Dissection
column2:
Answer: D. Dissection

Feedback:
The history of a recent MVA should lead to a possible diagnosis of either a pseudoaneurysm or dissection. The images displayed
demonstrate an intimal flap with flow reversal. Note the distinct separation of layers and flow direction during the two phases
of the cardiac cycle.

column1:
A patient presents with left arm paresthesia and intermittent blurring of vision in the right eye. These clinical findings are most consistent with:

A: RT PCA stenosis
B: LT PCA stenosis
C: LT ICA stenosis
D: RT ICA stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. RT ICA stenosis

Feedback:
Extremity paresthesia can be a sign of ICA stenosis. The right ICA feeds the part of the brain that controls the left side of the
body. Visual symptoms can be sign of ICA stenosis. The ophthalmic artery is a branch of the ICA and supplies the eye with blood.
If the right ICA is blocked, it will affect flow in the right ophthalmic artery and right eye.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes carotid dissection?

A: Dissection is more common in the Circle of Willis than the extracranial arteries
B: Commonly associated with Paget Schroetter syndrome
C: Dissection is more common in the extracranial ICA than the intracranial ICA
D: Always associated with trauma
column2:
Answer: C. Dissection is more common in the extracranial ICA than the intracranial ICA

Feedback:
Dissection is more common in the extracranial ICA than the intracranial ICA

column1:
What is the minimum amount of diameter stenosis in the internal carotid artery that will demonstrate hemodynamic significance?

A: 75%
B: 25%
C: 95%
D: 50%
column2:
Answer: D. 50%

Feedback:
Hemodynamic significance refers to the appearance of signs and symptoms related to the compromised blood flow. 50%
diameter or 75% area stenosis of a vessel is described as hemodynamically significant.

column1:
What findings are displayed on the image?

A: partial subclavian steal on the left side
B: bilateral normal vertebral waveforms
C: partial subclavian steal on the right side
D: abnormal left waveform associated with temporal arteritis
column2:
Answer: A. partial subclavian steal on the left side

Feedback:
Flow in the left vertebral artery is reversed. Some flow reversal is present in diastole which indicates a partial steal. If the flow is
reversed throughout the cardiac cycle, a complete steal is present.

column1:
Which of the following describes internalization of the ECA flow?

A: The ECA will have a low resistance Doppler flow pattern similar to the waveform in a normal ICA.
B: Anatomic variation of the ECA where it is found within the cerebral tissues and connects to the circle of Willis, similar to the distal ICA.
C: The left ECA will have a high resistance Doppler flow pattern similar to the ICA.
D: The ECA and ICA are transposed anatomically. The ICA has many branches with a smaller caliber and the ECA is larger with no visible branches.
column2:
Answer: A. The ECA will have a low resistance Doppler flow pattern similar to the waveform in a normal ICA.

Feedback:
The conversion of the ECA flow to more of an ICA flow pattern can occur with ICA occlusion and ECA collateral formation. If the
ECA takes over the supply of the cerebral tissues, the flow within becomes low resistance with increased diastolic flow to
accommodate the new organ that is now being supplied.

column1:
The PW Doppler tracing displays what level of ICA stenosis?

A: 0-49%
B: 50-69%
C: 70-99%
D: 100%
column2:
Answer: C. 70-99%

Feedback:
The peak velocity appears to reach the top of the scale at 450cm/s. Each slash mark on the velocity scale indicates 75cm/s. The
estimated end diastolic velocity appears to be about 140cm/s. These velocities indicate an 70-99% stenosis.

column1:
The image demonstrated is from a routine carotid ultrasound. Based on this image, what should be done next to evaluate this patient?

A: Perform a TCD exam to look for a contralateral MCA for obstruction
B: Perform a TCD exam to look for a basilar obstruction
C: Evaluate the left subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
D: Evaluate the right subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
column2:
Answer: B. Perform a TCD exam to look for a basilar obstruction

Feedback:
When a normally low resistance waveform is now triphasic, it indicates a distal obstruction near to the sampling site. The high
resistance triphasic waveform in a cerebrovascular artery indicates a distal obstruction. This waveform in the vertebral artery
indicates a distal obstruction, which could be the basilar artery. The vertebral arteries merge to form the basilar artery. A TCD
exam could identify an obstruction in the basilar artery that would explain this vertebral waveform. If the other vertebral artery
is also high resistance, this would also support a basilar obstruction.

column1:
Which of the following will result in a low resistance antegrade external carotid artery waveform with increased antegrade flow in diastole?

A: ipsilateral mid common carotid artery occlusion
B: temporal arteritis
C: contralateral subclavian stenosis
D: ipsilateral mid common carotid artery stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. ipsilateral mid common carotid artery stenosis

Feedback:
The ECA normally demonstrates a high resistance flow pattern with minimal antegrade diastolic flow. A proximal stenosis will
cause reduced resistance in the flow/waveforms distally. Stenosis proximal to the ECA (in the CCA) or in the proximal ECA will
cause lower resistance ECA flow with increased antegrade flow in diastole. If the ipsilateral ICA is occluded, these same changes
will occur.

column1:
What is the expected waveform characteristic if the Doppler sample volume is placed at the same location as the calipers?

A: triphasic with increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
B: blunted waveform with increased diastolic flow
C: increased peak systolic velocity and decreased end diastolic velocity
D: monophasic with increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
column2:
Answer: D. monophasic with increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocities

Feedback:
The calculated diameter stenosis is 76% which indicates a severe stenosis. The waveform at the stenosis will indicate a
monophasic waveform with increased systolic and diastolic flow pattern.

column1:
Which of the following is not part of the standard TCD protocol for vasospasm?

A: Perform serial exams to evaluate flow velocity in the MCA
B: Calculate the Lindegaard Ratio
C: Report all mean flow velocities in the MCA that are greater than 120cm/s
D: Measure any anechoic spaces around the brain that could indicate a hemorrhage
column2:
Answer: D. Measure any anechoic spaces around the brain that could indicate a hemorrhage

Feedback:
A TCD exam for vasospasm involves performing serial evaluations of the MCA velocities. Report all mean flow velocities in the
MCA that are greater than 120cm/s because this indicates a positive exam. The Lindegaard Ratio is calculated by dividing the
mean MCA velocity by the mean ICA velocity. A ratio >3 indicates a positive exam. There is no 2D imaging in a TCD exam,
therefore the cerebral tissue cannot be evaluated.

column1:
The findings on the image are most suggestive of: view image

A: pulmonary hypertension
B: aortic valve stenosis
C: malignant hypertension
D: mitral valve stenosis
column2:
Answer: B. aortic valve stenosis

Feedback:
Aortic Valve Stenosis is usually caused by degenerative disease, but can be caused by congenital malformation of the aortic
valve (bicuspid AV). Blood flow increases in velocity as it moves through the narrowed valve. The flow into the ascending aorta
will be turbulent. Characteristics of post-stenotic flow can be transmitted to the carotid arteries: lower resistance, turbulence
and reduced velocity. Brachial pressures may also be lower than normal. The ICA/CCA systolic and diastolic velocity ratios
should be used to assess the patient for carotid stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following will not demonstrate a bruit on auscultation?

A: occlusion
B: stenosis
C: AV fistula
D: pseudoaneurysm
column2:
Answer: A. occlusion

Feedback:
An occlusion will not demonstrate a bruit because no flow is moving under the stethoscope.

column1:
A patient that experienced a fall from 15ft yesterday presents with acute hemiparesis and dysphasia. These clinical findings are most suggestive of:

A: spontaneous intracerebral aneurysm rupture
B: carotid dissection
C: polycythemia vera
D: basilar artery rupture
column2:
Answer: B. carotid dissection

Feedback:
A carotid dissection should be suspected with trauma. Spontaneous intracerebral aneurysm rupture and basilar artery rupture
would cause critical loss of blood within the cranium. In most cases death would be eminent within hours. At 36hrs post trauma,
these two diagnoses are very unlikely.

column1:
What is causing the waveform displayed?

A: proximal stenosis
B: subclavian steal
C: arrhythmia
D: distal stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. arrhythmia

Feedback:
Note the difference in timing of the systolic peaks of the waveform. This indicates a cardiac arrhythmia. In this case atrial
fibrillation is present. The velocity demonstrated is normal for the vertebral arteries.

column1:
If both carotid waveforms look like the image above, you should check the patient chart to see if they have: view image

A: constrictive pericarditis
B: subclavian steal
C: an intra-aortic balloon pump
D: severe aortic stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. an intra-aortic balloon pump

Feedback:
IABP assisted waveforms have 2 systolic peaks for each pulse. The first peak indicates the left ventricular contraction. The
second peak indicates the balloon inflation. EDV cannot be determined in the patients with an IABP because flow reversal is
commonly seen after the balloon deflation. If possible, turn off the IABP during carotid evaluation. If this is not possible, velocity
from the first peak should be used as the PSV.
Image Credit
Adapted from: Ginat, D., Massey, H. T., Bhatt, S., & Dogra, V. S. (2011). Imaging of mechanical cardiac assist devices. Journal of
clinical imaging science, 1, 21. doi:10.4103/2156-7514.80373 Copyright-open license

column1: Select the area of the color display where the largest spectral window would be demonstrated on PW Doppler evaluation. Click to set the cursor and confirm your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
Large spectral window = laminar flow. The area of the common carotid artery that most proximal to the stenosis would
demonstrate the most laminar flow. Laminar flow is also best displayed from the center of the vessel. Spectral broadening will
occur at the stenosis and distal to the stenosis due to turbulence.

column1:
Bilateral tardus parvus waveforms in the carotid arteries can indicate:

A: pulmonary regurgitation
B: mitral stenosis
C: pulmonary stenosis
D: mitral regurgitation
column2:
Answer: B. mitral stenosis

Feedback:
Significant stenosis can cause left atrial pressure overload and reduced cardiac output. The carotid arteries will demonstrate
low resistance, low velocity flow with reduced cardiac output. Bilateral tardus parvus waveforms in the carotid arteries can
indicate severe mitral stenosis.

column1:
Which of the following conditions would cause an increase in the systemic BP in just the right arm?

A: aortic coarctation just distal to the origin of the left common carotid artery
B: right subclavian steal
C: thoracic outlet syndrome of the right arm
D: right subclavian artery stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. aortic coarctation just distal to the origin of the left common carotid artery

Feedback:
If aortic coarctation occurs just distal to the origin of the left common carotid artery, BP will be reduced in the left arm and
increased in the right arm. BP will be reduced in the right arm with the other choices.

column1:
This patient presents with a bruit, palpable mass, mild SOB and dizziness. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: AV fistula
B: Pseudoaneurysm
C: Carotid body tumor
D: Thyroid mass
column2:
Answer: C. Carotid body tumor

Feedback:
With only a 2D image, a diagnosis regarding the presence or absence of stenosis cannot be accurately made. The arrow
indicates a large mass at the CCA bifurcation that surrounds the ICA. This is the classic appearance and location for a carotid
body tumor. The symptoms listed are commonly seen with this type of tumor. The thyroid is located much more distally in the
neck, therefore a thyroid mass is very unlikely.

column1:
The PW Doppler tracing was obtained from the CCA. Which statement below is true regarding the image?

A: Normal high resistance, triphasic flow is demonstrated
B: Abnormal high resistance flow due to increased flow in Vertebral supply
C: Abnormal high resistance flow due to ICA occlusion
D: Abnormal high resistance flow due to proximal stenosis
column2:
Answer: C. Abnormal high resistance flow due to ICA occlusion

Feedback:
Note the flow reversal in diastole with no antegrade flow during the full diastolic portion of the cycle. This indicates significant
resistance distal to the area evaluated. An occluded ICA can lead to the increase in resistance in the CCA which causes the
waveform type displayed on the image.

column1:
Regarding the image below, where will Reynold's number be the highest?

A: immediately proximal to the caliper location
B: immediately distal to the caliper location
C: in the CCA bulb
D: distal ICA
column2:
Answer: B. immediately distal to the caliper location

Feedback:
Flow in the arterial segment just distal to the stenosis will demonstrate the greatest amount of turbulence and highest
Reynold's number. Flow with a Reynold's number over 2000 is considered turbulent.

column1:
Which of the following is a characteristic of an ICA waveform with 50- 69% stenosis?

A: diastolic flow reversal
B: increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocity
C: clear spectral window
D: decreased diastolic flow with EDV less than 20cm/s
column2:
Answer: B. increased peak systolic and end diastolic velocity

Feedback:
Stenosis in the ICA leads to increased systolic and diastolic flow velocities. Spectral broadening occurs with stenosis. Diastolic
flow reversal in the ICA is identified with an occlusion distal to the Doppler sample.

column1:
Which of the following would cause flow in the left ECA to reverse direction during systole?

A: right CCA occlusion
B: left vertebral artery occlusion
C: left CCA occlusion
D: left proximal ICA occlusion
column2:
Answer: C. left CCA occlusion

Feedback:
Ipsilateral CCA occlusion can cause ECA flow reversal so that it can supply the ICA with flow. Collateral flow from the
contralateral cerebral system will back fill the left ECA. Reversed flow in the ECA will fill the left ICA with blood because the CCA
is occluded.

column1:
Aside from the carotid artery, what other vessels are commonly affected by fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD)?

A: jugular veins
B: small arteries of the hand and wrist
C: renal and mesenteric arteries
D: small arteries of the foot and ankle
column2:
Answer: C. renal and mesenteric arteries

Feedback:
If FMD is identified in the carotid arteries, the renal and mesenteric arteries should also be evaluated for FMD. Stenosis caused
by atherosclerosis usually affects the origin of the artery, while FMD usually causes stenosis in the mid/distal artery.

column1:
Which cardiac abnormality usually has NO effect on the peak systolic velocities in the carotid arteries?

A: aortic valve stenosis
B: coronary artery disease
C: mitral valve regurgitation
D: cardiomyopathy
column2:
Answer: C. mitral valve regurgitation

Feedback:
Cardiac structure and function should be considered on all vascular evaluations. Changes in cardiac function can affect the
velocities in the arterial system and change the flow characteristics of the venous system. Mitral regurgitation does NOT affect
the systolic velocities in the arterial system. Aortic stenosis, mitral stenosis, CHF, diastolic dysfunction, dilated cardiomyopathy
and CAD can reduce the cardiac ejection fraction and falsely reduce the PSV in the arterial system. Systemic HTN, aortic
regurgitation, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and compensatory heart beats with arrhythmias can falsely elevate the PSV.

column1:
You are performing a TCD exam to assess collateral flow with a right ICA occlusion. You identify flow moving away from the transducer at 90cm/s with decreased pulsatility in the right ophthalmic artery. The left ophthalmic artery demonstrates flow moving toward the transducer with a velocity of 45cm/s. What is indicated by these findings?

A: ECA to ICA collateralization
B: Posterior to anterior collateralization
C: Cross over collateralization
D: Normal flow parameters, no collateralization present
column2:
Answer: A. ECA to ICA collateralization

Feedback:
The superficial temporal artery and the inferior maxillary artery are branches of the ECA that communicate with branches of the
ophthalmic artery. Flow moves from the ECA to these branches and refills the ophthalmic artery to send flow back into the
areas affected by the ipsilateral ICA occlusion.

column1:
What cardiac complications are associated with the findings on the image? view image

A: aortic regurgitation or pulmonary regurgitation
B: mitral stenosis or pulmonary stenosis
C: coronary artery disease or aortic stenosis
D: cardiac tamponade or constrictive pericarditis
column2:
Answer: D. cardiac tamponade or constrictive pericarditis

Feedback:
Pulsus Alternans - the peak systolic velocity alternates between two levels with each heartbeat. Pulsus Alternans is associated
with cardiac tamponade, dilated cardiomyopathy and constrictive pericarditis

column1:
The external carotid artery normally demonstrates a velocity below:

A: 100cm/s
B: 80cm/s
C: 150cm/s
D: 230cm/s
column2:
Answer: C. 150cm/s

Feedback:
The external carotid artery normally demonstrates a velocity below 150 cm/s

column1:
Which of the following terms is a type of visual disturbance where half of the field of view is disrupted in the eye?

A: ectopia
B: homonymous hemianopia
C: myopia
D: diplopia
column2:
Answer: B. homonymous hemianopia

Feedback:
This visual disturbance can be unilateral or bilateral and is commonly caused by ischemia in the occipital lobe of the brain.
Medial or lateral half of the field of view in the eye is "absent" while the other half appears normal.

column1:
Aspirin would be the preferred treatment for which of the following patients?

A: 60% internal carotid stenosis
B: total occlusion of an internal carotid artery
C: presence of significant venous reflux
D: compartment syndrome
column2:
Answer: A. 60% internal carotid stenosis

Feedback:
Aspirin prevents platelet aggregation in the arteries which reduces the ability of plaque to form. Patients with mild to moderate
carotid stenosis can benefit from an aspirin each day.

column1:
Which of the following defines a stroke?

A: nerve conduction abnormalities within the cerebral tissue
B: acute ischemia in cerebral tissue
C: occlusion of the internal carotid artery
D: mobile piece of plaque or thrombus
column2:
Answer: B. acute ischemia in cerebral tissue

Feedback:
Loss of blood flow to an area of cerebral tissue causes cerebral ischemia. All strokes are not caused by ICA occlusion and not
every patient with an occluded ICA has a stroke.

column1:
The red arrows on the image are pointing to what abnormal characteristic of the Doppler waveform?

A: Formation of a spectral window
B: Vibrations from post stenotic turbulence
C: Temporal tap response
D: Crosstalk artifact
column2:
Answer: B. Vibrations from post stenotic turbulence

Feedback:
The arrows indicate varied flow velocities seen in post-stenotic turbulence. Note the multiple and numerous velocity spikes
throughout the tracing and the mosaic color displayed on the guiding image. The ICA does not typically respond to the temporal
tap maneuver. The rate of spiking would not be possible with normal tapping for the maneuver.

column1:
What is the most common location of an intracranial aneurysm?

A: Anterior communicating artery
B: Middle cerebral artery
C: Internal carotid artery siphon
D: Posterior communicating artery
column2:
Answer: A. Anterior communicating artery

Feedback:
The anterior communicating artery is the most common location for a berry or saccular intracranial aneurysm.

column1:
Which of the following is a characteristic of the Doppler waveform obtained at the site of stenosis in an ICA with 80% stenosis?

A: spectral window
B: blunted waveform
C: decreased acceleration time
D: increased diastolic flow
column2:
Answer: D. increased diastolic flow

Feedback:
As stenosis increases, peak systolic and end diastolic velocities increase. Acceleration time increases as stenosis increases. As
the vessel nears 99% stenosis, systolic and diastolic flow may be significantly reduced. Occlusion will lead to increased
resistance in the proximal vessel.

column1:
Broca aphasia can be defined as:

A: Inability to swallow liquids
B: Inability to understand speech, speak, or follow directions
C: Inability to speak but can understand and follow directions
D: Inability to swallow solid food
column2:
Answer: C. Inability to speak but can understand and follow directions

Feedback:
Broca aphasia (AKA expressive aphasia) can be defined as the inability to speak but can understand and follow directions.

column1:
Which of the following is a potential symptom caused by a subclavian steal?

A: systemic HTN
B: unilateral amaurosis fugax
C: pulmonary HTN
D: syncope
column2:
Answer: D. syncope

Feedback:
Vertebrobasilar disease is associated with syncope. Amaurosis fugax is a visual disturbance than can be related to ICA stenosis.
HTN is a risk factor for developing vascular disease.

column1:
If the left proximal CCA demonstrates a PSV of 35cm/s and the right proximal CCA demonstrates a PSV of 115cm/s, which of the following could explain the findings?

A: aortic valve stenosis
B: left ICA occlusion
C: congestive heart failure
D: decreased hematocrit levels
column2:
Answer: B. left ICA occlusion

Feedback:
If a single CCA demonstrates elevated velocity = tortuosity on that side or compensatory flow due to obstruction in the other
carotid system. If the left ICA is occluded, flow in the CCA will be low velocity with a quick upstroke and little to no diastolic flow.
The right CCA velocity can increase as it tries to provide the additional cerebrovascular flow needed. The left vertebral artery
may also demonstrate elevated velocity as it tries to compensate for the ipsilateral ICA occlusion. The other choices would cause
bilateral hemodynamic changes.

column1:
A peak systolic velocity of 100 cm/s is an indication of ___________ in the ICA.

A: normal flow
B: mild stenosis
C: moderate stenosis
D: severe stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. normal flow

Feedback:
The peak systolic flow velocity in the ICA is normal when it is less than 125cm/s.

column1:
What is the best quantitative parameter to use to estimate internal carotid artery stenosis in patients with severe aortic valve stenosis?

A: end diastolic velocity
B: velocity ratio
C: acceleration time
D: peak systolic and end diastolic velocities
column2:
Answer: B. velocity ratio

Feedback:
Flow in the carotid arteries will be diminished in patients with significant aortic valve stenosis. When cardiac output is reduced,
the flow velocities in the carotid systems will be reduced. This can lead to underestimation of ICA stenosis. The velocity ratio is
used to provide a more accurate of estimate stenosis in these patients.

column1:
Which of the following describes collateral flow from the ECA to the ICA on the same side of the body?

A: posterior communicator to the basilar artery
B: superior thyroidal artery to the ACA
C: lingual artery to the MCA
D: superficial temporal artery to the ophthalmic artery
column2:
Answer: D. superficial temporal artery to the ophthalmic artery

Feedback:
Branches of the superficial temporal artery meet with branches of the supraorbital artery. Retrograde flow in the supraorbital
artery will enter the ophthalmic artery, reversed flow will be identified in the ophthalmic artery. Flow will be moving back toward
the ICA instead of outward toward the eye.

column1:
Which of the following scenarios demonstrates the highest risk for embolization?

A: Buerger disease
B: chronic ICA occlusion
C: 60% ICA stenosis with moderate, smooth calcific atheroma
D: 40% ICA stenosis with mild, irregular soft atheroma
column2:
Answer: D. 40% ICA stenosis with mild, irregular soft atheroma

Feedback:
Stenosis of the internal carotid artery carries the highest risk of TIA. Soft plaque has a much higher risk of embolization than the
"fixed" calcific atherosclerosis.

column1:
The most accurate Doppler predictor of ICA stenosis is:

A: the end diastolic velocity
B: the systolic ratio (ICAs/CCAs)
C: the diastolic ratio (ICAd/CCAd)
D: the peak systolic velocity
column2:
Answer: D. the peak systolic velocity

Feedback:
Recent studies have shown that the most accurate Doppler predictor of ICA stenosis is the peak systolic velocity.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound due to recent episodes of near syncope. Noticeable respiratory variation in the peak velocities of the carotid arteries is present. What cardiac complication should be suspected?

A: cardiac tamponade
B: tricuspid stenosis
C: low cardiac output
D: mitral stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. cardiac tamponade

Feedback:
Cardiac tamponade is a critical finding that requires pericardiocentesis. Severe pericardial effusion within the fibrous
pericardium causes extrinsic compression of the right heart chambers. Respiratory variation in arterial flow velocities will occur.
Flow diminishes with inspiration and normalizes with expiration. Another sign of cardiac tamponade is pulsus alternans. Pulsus
Alternans - the peak systolic velocity alternates between two levels with each beat. Pulsus Alternans is also associated with
dilated cardiomyopathy and constrictive pericarditis.

column1:
The image demonstrates the flow pattern taken in the right vertebral artery. Which of the following best describes the findings?

A: Probable stenosis of the left subclavian artery
B: Probable stenosis of the right subclavian artery
C: The vertebral vein flow is demonstrated not the artery.
D: There is an obstruction distally in the intracranial circulation
column2:
Answer: B. Probable stenosis of the right subclavian artery

Feedback:
The waveform demonstrates early systolic deceleration and is called "pre-steal waveform". This is a sign of a proximal stenosis
of the subclavian artery. Provocative maneuvers can be used to evaluate the ability to convert the waveform to a partial or
complete steal. Arm exercise followed by a repeat scan. Pneumatic cuff inflation on the affected arm for 2-3 minutes followed
by rapid release and repeat scan.

column1:
Which of the following is a characteristic of the Doppler waveform obtained distal to the site of stenosis in an ICA that demonstrates string flow on color Doppler?

A: spectral window
B: blunted waveform
C: increased diastolic flow
D: decreased acceleration time
column2:
Answer: B. blunted waveform

Feedback:
Near occlusion of the ICA will lead to low velocity systolic flow with minimal diastolic flow (blunted waveform). Once total
occlusion occurs, diastolic flow ceases or flow reversal occurs in the artery just proximal to the occlusion.

column1:
The image demonstrates the flow pattern taken in the right vertebral artery. What should be done next to better evaluate these findings

A: Inflate a pneumatic cuff on the left arm to a level of 20mmHg above systolic pressure, wait 2 minutes to rapidly deflate and rescan
B: Have the patient perform the Valsalva maneuver and rescan during the strain
C: Have the patient perform the Valsalva maneuver and rescan during the release
D: Inflate a pneumatic cuff on the right arm to a level of 20mmHg above systolic pressure, wait 2 minutes to rapidly deflate and rescan
column2:
Answer: D. Inflate a pneumatic cuff on the right arm to a level of 20mmHg above systolic pressure, wait 2 minutes to rapidly deflate and rescan

Feedback:
The waveform demonstrates early systolic deceleration and is called "pre-steal waveform". This is a sign of a proximal stenosis
of the subclavian artery. Provocative maneuvers can be used to evaluate the ability to convert the waveform to a partial or
complete steal. Arm exercise followed by a repeat scan. Pneumatic cuff inflation on the affected arm for 2-3 minutes followed
by rapid release and repeat scan.

column1:
Which of the following is a condition that cannot be evaluated with Transcranial Doppler?

A: MCA disease
B: vertebro-basilar disease
C: patent foramen ovale
D: temporal arteritis
column2:
Answer: D. temporal arteritis

Feedback:
The temporal artery is located extracranially and requires a high frequency probe to evaluate the superficial vessel. A PFO can
be evaluated using microbubbles to assess shunt flow in the heart. Microbubbles injected into the venous circulation should not
end up in the arterial vessels of the brain. An ASD must be present if the air embolisms are identified as turbulent spikes in the
Doppler tracing of the intracranial arteries.

column1:
The string flow sign is associated with which of the following diagnoses?

A: critical ICA stenosis
B: fibromuscular dysplasia
C: AV fistula
D: superficial phlebitis
column2:
Answer: A. critical ICA stenosis

Feedback:
The string sign refers to a critical stenosis that only allows a thin string of blood through the tiny opening. The flow velocity will
be very low with decreased pulsatility. The risk for total occlusion is extremely high in these cases. FMD causes the appearance
of a string of beads.

column1:
Which of the following would lead to underestimation of stenosis in the internal carotid artery?

A: Short area of focal stenosis
B: Significant aortic valve stenosis
C: Elevated heart rate
D: Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side
column2:
Answer: B. Significant aortic valve stenosis

Feedback:
Underestimation of stenosis in the ICA can be seen with:
Reduced systemic blood pressure
Proximal stenosis - such as significant aortic valve stenosis
Long area of focal stenosis
Tandem stenosis or eccentric stenosis
Critical stenosis (string flow)
Reduced cardiac output - CHF, coronary artery disease, dilated cardiomyopathy

column1:
Which of the following terms would be used to describe flow identified just proximal to a 95% ICA stenosis?

A: tardus parvus
B: low resistance
C: damped
D: laminar
column2:
Answer: C. damped

Feedback:
Pre-Stenotic Zone:
Increased resistance and resistive index
Flow velocity may be damped with loss of diastolic flow
Short acceleration time
Called stump or thump flow
Note the first waveform on the image below. Low velocity with little to no diastolic flow. There is a critical stenosis of the ICA
which causes increased resistance to flow proximally.

column1:
Which of the following demonstrates the correct calculation for the Lindegaard Ratio?

A: siphon peak systolic velocity divided by proximal ICA peak systolic velocity
B: MCA mean flow velocity divided by siphon peak systolic velocity
C: MCA mean flow velocity divided by distal ICA mean flow velocity
D: ACA peak systolic velocity divided by distal ICA peak systolic velocity
column2:
Answer: C. MCA mean flow velocity divided by distal ICA mean flow velocity

Feedback:
The Lindegaard Ratio (Hemispheric index) is calculated using the MCA mean flow velocity and dividing it by the distal ICA mean
flow velocity. It is used to evaluate a suspected vasospasm and/or hyperperfusion.

column1:
What cardiac complication is related to the appearance of the carotid waveform? view image

A: aortic regurgitation
B: pulmonary stenosis
C: mitral regurgitation
D: cardiac tamponade
column2:
Answer: A. aortic regurgitation

Feedback:
Blood moves back through the aortic valve into the left ventricle during diastole. It is usually caused by diseased valve,
congenital defects and chamber or aortic dilatation. Significant regurgitation results in volume overload in the chamber
receiving the retrograde blood flow. With aortic regurgitation, volume overload occurs in the left ventricle. Pulsus bisferiens can
occur in the carotid arteries. The waveforms will demonstrate double systolic peaks. Severe regurgitation is associated with
diastolic flow reversal in the carotid arteries (Water Hammer Pulse).

column1: Select the area of the color display where the highest systolic velocity would be demonstrated on PW Doppler evaluation. Click to set the cursor and confirm your answer.
column2:
Answer: Unavailable

Feedback:
The highest velocity of flow is seen within the stenosis and just as the blood exits the most narrowed area. When evaluating a
suspected stenosis, it is important to take several samples with small adjustments to the position of the sample volume in order
to locate the highest velocities present.

column1:
A pulsatility index of > 1.2 in the cerebral vessels indicates:

A: normal flow resistance
B: vasospasm
C: mildly decreased resistance
D: severely decreased resistance
column2:
Answer: B. vasospasm

Feedback:
The greater the PI, the greater the variation of velocity throughout the cardiac cycle. The greater the PI, the higher the resistance
to flow. Cerebral vessels should normally have a low PI value. Vasospasm causes increased resistance in cerebral vessels and an
increased PI value.

column1:
The bilateral proximal common carotid arteries demonstrate normal velocity and systolic upstroke. Diastolic flow reversal is present and continues throughout diastole. These findings are most consistent with:

A: significant aortic valve regurgitation
B: mid CCA occlusion
C: mild to moderate aortic valve stenosis
D: proximal ICA occlusion
column2:
Answer: A. significant aortic valve regurgitation

Feedback:
Significant aortic valve regurgitation will cause flow to move from the ascending aorta/arch into the left ventricle during diastole.
This can cause flow reversal that lasts throughout diastole in the branches of the arch. Both CCA will be affected. The flow
pattern is called a Water Hammer Pulse.
If there was an ICA occlusion, the CCA velocity would be reduced with minimal diastolic flow reversal in early diastole.

column1:
You perform a bilateral carotid Doppler and there is very little plaque formation. All other waveforms appear within normal limits, but the mid and distal left ICA segments demonstrate the waveform on the image. What is the best way to evaluate the patient based on these findings?

A: TCD exam to evaluate the left carotid siphon
B: TCD exam to evaluate the vertebrobasilar system
C: TCD exam to evaluate the right carotid siphon
D: TCD exam to evaluate the direction of flow in the anterior cerebral communicator artery
column2:
Answer: A. TCD exam to evaluate the left carotid siphon

Feedback:
High resistance flow in the ICA indicates increased resistance downstream. Obstruction of the siphon can cause increased
resistance in the mid/distal ICA.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes the standard protocol for performing cerebral angiography?

A: the Seldinger technique is used to puncture the common femoral artery in order to insert a catheter that will be advanced to the left ventricle for contrast administration
B: the Seldinger technique is used to puncture the common femoral artery in order to insert a catheter that will be advanced to the aortic arch for contrast administration
C: the Seldinger technique is used to puncture the brachial artery in order to insert a catheter that will be advanced to the left ventricle for contrast administration
D: the Seldinger technique is used to puncture the common carotid artery and inject contrast through a catheter
column2:
Answer: B. the Seldinger technique is used to puncture the common femoral artery in order to insert a catheter that will be advanced to the aortic arch for contrast administration

Feedback:
The Seldinger technique is used to puncture the common femoral artery in order to insert a catheter that will be advanced to
the aortic arch for contrast administration. Contrast is released and enters the aortic arch branches to fill both carotid arteries.
If the catheter is inserted into one of the carotid arteries, contrast would only fill that side of the system. Catheter insertion into
the brachial artery would fill vessels distal to the puncture site = forearm/hand. Catheter insertion into the femoral artery would
fill vessels distal to the puncture site = leg/foot.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid Doppler exam due to left arm and leg paresthesia. Which vessel should be closely evaluated for a potential vascular cause of the symptom?

A: left ICA
B: left vertebral
C: right vertebral
D: right ICA
column2:
Answer: D. right ICA

Feedback:
Symptoms of stroke that occur in the body are associated with ICA disease on the contralateral side. Left extremity paresthesia
would be associated with right ICA disease.

column1:
A bilateral visual symptom, such as diplopia, is commonly associated with disease in which artery?

A: Anterior Cerebral
B: Vertebral
C: Posterior Cerebral
D: Innominate
column2:
Answer: B. Vertebral

Feedback:
In reference to symptoms related to cranial vessel disease, unilateral visual disturbances are most commonly associated with
disease in the ICA. Bilateral visual disturbances are most commonly associated with vertebrobasilar disease.

column1:
Vertigo is a common symptom related to disease of which of the following vessels?

A: basilar artery
B: anterior cerebral artery
C: internal carotid artery
D: middle cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: A. basilar artery

Feedback:
The cerebellum is responsible for helping to maintain balance and equilibrium. The vertebral and basilar arteries give off
branches that supply the cerebellum. If flow is reduced in these arteries, cerebellar ischemia can occur leading to vertigo.

column1:
What is the preferred manipulation technique that can be used to convert the partial subclavian steal into a complete steal?

A: Re-scan the patient in Fowler's position.
B: Have the patient squeeze a stress ball 40 times in the right arm and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.
C: Have the patient perform 40 flexion/extension maneuvers of the left arm and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.
D: Have patient sit up in an exaggerated military stance and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.
column2:
Answer: C. Have the patient perform 40 flexion/extension maneuvers of the left arm and then re-evaluate the vertebral flow.

Feedback:
By exercising the left arm, the muscles will require more incoming blood flow. The pressure in the distal arm will decrease to
allow more flow to travel to the arm. The left vertebral artery is the only supplying vessel for the left arm, due to the subclavian
occlusion. This increase in blood flow needs in the left arm will lead to complete reversal of flow in the left vertebral as it tries to
keep up with the change in the blood flow needs in the left arm.

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the images displayed?

A: The increased PSV in the CCA is due to a single site of stenosis causing increased velocity and turbulence.
B: There is focal atherosclerosis formation within the distal CCA.
C: The Doppler tracing indicates laminar flow.
D: The increase in PSV in the CCA is due to the multiple adjacent lesions causing increased resistance.
column2:
Answer: D. The increase in PSV in the CCA is due to the multiple adjacent lesions causing increased resistance.

Feedback:
Note the diffuse atheroma formation along the length of the CCA which causes a combined effect on the flow velocity within the
vessel.

column1:
Which of the following patients has the lowest risk of a future stroke that affects the right side?

A: 60% left ICA stenosis caused by heterogeneous calcific plaque
B: no appreciable plaque identified in the left ICA
C: total occlusion of the left ICA
D: 20% left ICA stenosis caused by smooth, soft plaque
column2:
Answer: C. total occlusion of the left ICA

Feedback:
A stenosis on the left internal carotid artery will cause symptoms on the right side of the body. The risk of a future stroke on
that side drops significantly when the internal carotid artery is totally occluded. If a stroke would occur, it would have happened
at the time the occlusion occurred.

column1:
Which cerebral vessel is most commonly involved in an acute cerebral ischemia?

A: Posterior cerebral artery
B: Middle cerebral artery
C: basilar artery
D: Anterior cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: B. Middle cerebral artery

Feedback:
The MCA is most commonly involved in an acute occlusion and cerebral ischemia due to its connection to the ICA.

column1:
The image demonstrated is from a routine carotid ultrasound. Based on this image, what should be done next to evaluate this patient?

A: Evaluate the left vertebral to see if the waveform is also high resistance
B: Perform a TCD exam to look for a contralateral MCA for obstruction
C: Evaluate the right subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
D: Evaluate the left subclavian artery for the presence of monophasic flow
column2:
Answer: A. Evaluate the left vertebral to see if the waveform is also high resistance

Feedback:
The high resistance triphasic waveform in the vertebral artery indicate a distal obstruction. If the left vertebral is also triphasic,
there is most likely a basilar obstruction. If the left vertebral is normal and monophasic, there must be a distal vertebral
obstruction on the right side. A TCD exam could identify an obstruction in the basilar artery that would explain this vertebral
waveform.

column1:
What is the most common cause of cerebrovascular disease?

A: vasospasm
B: atherosclerosis
C: stroke
D: embolism
column2:
Answer: B. atherosclerosis

Feedback:
Atherosclerosis formation is the most common cause of cerebrovascular disease. Embolism formation is much less common
and is usually a complication of atherosclerosis formation. A stroke is a potential complication with all types of cerebrovascular
disease.

column1:
The carotid compression test is used to _______________, while the carotid sinus massage is used to ______________________.

A: increase flow in the vertebral arteries, decrease flow in the vertebral arteries
B: stimulate TIA symptoms, reduce TIA symptoms
C: reduce the heart rate, evaluate the presence of collateral flow
D: evaluate the presence of collateral flow, reduce the heart rate
column2:
Answer: D. evaluate the presence of collateral flow, reduce the heart rate

Feedback:
The carotid compression test is used to evaluate for the presence of collateral flow, while the carotid sinus massage is used to
reduce the heart rate to diagnose/treat certain arrhythmias. .

column1:
If the CCA PSV is 80cm/s and the CCA EDV is 40cm/s, what is the systolic velocity ratio?

A: 1.0
B: 2.1
C: 4.9
D: 9.7
column2:
Answer: C. 4.9

Feedback:
Systolic Velocity Ratio = PSV ICA / PSV CCA
388cm/s / 80 cm/s = 4.9
There should not be challenging calculations on the registry exam because you will not have access to a calculator. The
importance of this question is to know how to make the calculation.

column1:
What measurement is being taken in the image?

A: Continuity equation
B: Diameter stenosis
C: IMT
D: Area stenosis
column2:
Answer: D. Area stenosis

Feedback:
Area stenosis is calculated by measuring the area of the vessel and the true lumen .
Area 1 minus Area 2 divided by Area 1 X 100%
EX: 4cm2 - 2.5cm2 / 4cm2 = 0.375 = 38% stenosis

column1:
A patient presents with episodes of left eye amaurosis fugax and right sided paresthesia. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: 80% LT ECA stenosis
B: RT ICA occlusion
C: LT CCA stenosis proximal to current point of evaluation
D: LT ICA occlusion
column2:
Answer: D. LT ICA occlusion

Feedback:
The Left CCA waveform demonstrates very high resistance flow with flow reversal in diastole. This is indicative of a LT ICA
occlusion distally. The ipsilateral visual disturbance and contralateral limb paresthesia also support this diagnosis.

column1:
If there is a 90% stenosis in the left carotid siphon, which of the following is true?

A: left vertebral flow will be high resistance
B: left mid ICA flow will be high resistance
C: right MCA will be high resistance
D: left MCA flow will be high resistance
column2:
Answer: B. left mid ICA flow will be high resistance

Feedback:
Distal stenosis will lead to high resistance flow proximally = ICA high resistance with siphon stenosis
Proximal stenosis will lead to low resistance flow distally = MCA and ACA lower resistance with siphon stenosis

column1:
Which of the following describes the path of an embolus?

A: a small piece of soft plaque breaks off from the carotid bulb and floats toward the brain
B: a small piece of thrombus breaks off from a thrombus in the right ventricle and floats toward the brain
C: a small piece of thrombus breaks off from a thrombus in the left ventricle and floats toward the lungs
D: a small piece of soft plaque breaks off from the carotid bulb and floats toward the lungs
column2:
Answer: A. a small piece of soft plaque breaks off from the carotid bulb and floats toward the brain

Feedback:
An embolus is a floating piece of debris, air bubble, thrombus or plaque that moves distally until it lodges in a small vessel.
Thrombus in the right ventricle will be sent to pulmonary artery and the lungs = pulmonary embolism. Thrombus in the left
ventricle can be sent to the brain through the aorta = stroke. An embolus in the carotid system will go to the brain.

column1:
Why is the carotid compression technique performed?

A: to evaluate the venous outflow of the intracranial vessels
B: to evaluate the effects of vasospasm
C: to evaluate the presence of intracranial collateral formation
D: to evaluate the carotid as a potential arterial graft
column2:
Answer: C. to evaluate the presence of intracranial collateral formation

Feedback:
Carotid Compression Test is performed by a PHYSICIAN ONLY!! Used to evaluate the risk for carotid treatment; Compress
unilateral CCA for approximately 5 minutes, if no symptoms occur, collaterals are present that are compensating for reduced
flow.

column1:
Bilateral bruits are detected in the proximal neck. This finding is most suggestive of:

A: Subclavian steal
B: Aortic valve stenosis
C: Polycythemia vera
D: CHF
column2:
Answer: B. Aortic valve stenosis

Feedback:
Bilateral carotid bruits and changes in both carotid flow patterns can be related to aortic stenosis. If the aortic valve is stenotic
causing increased flow velocity and turbulence, flow in both carotids will reflect this change and may demonstrate bilateral
bruits. CHF would lead to decreased flow through both carotids but a bruit would most likely NOT be identified due to slow
moving flow.

column1:
Which of the following would be used to describe a Doppler waveform taken in the mid ICA with a critical stenosis of the proximal segment?

A: increased pulsatility index
B: peaked
C: increased resistive index
D: damped
column2:
Answer: D. damped

Feedback:
The Doppler waveform will be blunted and significantly damped downstream with a significant obstruction proximal to the
point of insonation. There will be minimal diastolic flow, but no flow reversal.

column1:
If there is an occlusion at the proximal ICA, what will happen to the flow in the CCA?

A: Turbulent with decreased resistance/increased diastolic flow
B: No effect on CCA flow until bulb area
C: Damped with increased resistance/decreased diastolic flow
D: Damped with unaffected resistance
column2:
Answer: C. Damped with increased resistance/decreased diastolic flow

Feedback:
The ECA normally demonstrates high resistance systolic flow with minimal diastolic flow. The ICA usually demonstrates low
resistance systolic flow with increased diastolic flow. The CCA normally demonstrates a combined flow pattern from both of the
branches with resistance and diastolic flow at levels between the ECA and the ICA. If the ICA is occluded, the CCA will
demonstrate a flow pattern very similar to the ECA because it is now the only CCA branch and resistance to flow is increased.

column1:
What is the diameter stenosis of the vessel on the image?

A: 82%
B: 24%
C: 56%
D: 76%
column2:
Answer: D. 76%

Feedback:
(0.54 - 0.13 / 0.54) X 100% = 0.41 / 0.54 = 0.76 x 100% = 76%
There should not be challenging calculations on the registry exam because you will not have access to a calculator. The
importance of this question is to know how to make the calculation.

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound for pre-op testing for an aortic valve replacement surgery. The echo report in their chart indicates the stenosis is severe. What is the best way to determine if carotid stenosis is present in this patient?

A: using the peak systolic and end systolic velocity of the CCA
B: unable to evaluate carotid stenosis in patients with known heart disease
C: calculate the ICA/CCA systolic and diastolic velocity ratios
D: using the peak systolic and end systolic velocity of the ICA
column2:
Answer: C. calculate the ICA/CCA systolic and diastolic velocity ratios

Feedback:
With aortic valve stenosis, blood flow increases in velocity as it moves through the narrowed valve. The flow into the ascending
aorta will be turbulent. Characteristics of post-stenotic flow can be transmitted to the carotid arteries: lower resistance,
turbulence and reduced velocity. Brachial pressures may also be lower than normal. The ICA/CCA systolic and diastolic velocity
ratios should be used to assess the patient for carotid stenosis because peak velocities may be diminished.

column1:
The most common location of atherosclerotic formation in the cerebrovascular system?

A: vertebrobasilar system
B: circle of Willis
C: carotid bifurcation
D: ophthalmic and orbital arteries
column2:
Answer: C. carotid bifurcation

Feedback:
The most common location of atherosclerotic formation in the cerebrovascular system occurs at the carotid bifurcation.

column1:
An inpatient that is currently on Heparin therapy for a recent pulmonary embolism is referred for a carotid Duplex exam. The prior carotid exam from last year demonstrated mild plaque with normal flow velocities. On today's exam, the velocities in both carotid systems are elevated to an abnormal level, but the plaque formation appears unchanged. What could explain the change on today's exam?

A: Increased viscosity of the blood
B: Decreased hematocrit levels in the blood
C: Congestive heart failure has manifested
D: Intracranial occlusion causing collateral flow compensation from the extracranial arteries
column2:
Answer: B. Decreased hematocrit levels in the blood

Feedback:
Heparin is a blood thinner that will reduce the hematocrit (viscosity) levels of the blood. Decreased viscosity = Increased cardiac
output and velocity.

column1:
When a water hammer pulse is identified in the carotid arteries, what cardiac complication should be suspected?

A: severe aortic regurgitation
B: severe tricuspid regurgitation
C: severe mitral regurgitation
D: severe mitral stenosis
column2:
Answer: A. severe aortic regurgitation

Feedback:
Blood moves back through the aortic valve into the left ventricle during diastole. It is usually caused by diseased valve,
congenital defects and chamber or aortic dilatation. Significant regurgitation results in volume overload in the chamber
receiving the retrograde blood flow. With aortic regurgitation, volume overload occurs in the left ventricle. Pulsus bisferiens can
occur in the carotid arteries. The waveforms will demonstrate double systolic peaks. Severe regurgitation is associated with
diastolic flow reversal in the carotid arteries (Water Hammer Pulse).

column1:
Pulsus bisferiens in the carotid arteries is a sign of:

A: aortic regurgitation
B: mitral regurgitation
C: mitral stenosis
D: pulmonary regurgitation
column2:
Answer: A. aortic regurgitation

Feedback:
Blood moves back through the aortic valve into the left ventricle during diastole. It is usually caused by diseased valve,
congenital defects and chamber or aortic dilatation. Significant regurgitation results in volume overload in the chamber
receiving the retrograde blood flow. With aortic regurgitation, volume overload occurs in the left ventricle. Pulsus bisferiens can
occur in the carotid arteries. The waveforms will demonstrate double systolic peaks. Severe regurgitation is associated with
diastolic flow reversal in the carotid arteries (Water Hammer Pulse).

column1:
While scanning through the suboccipital window with midline angulation, the mean flow velocity increases from 55cm/s to 145cm/s at 105mm in depth. What do these findings mean?

A: Normal velocity changes for the tortuous carotid siphon
B: Significant stenosis is present in the basilar artery
C: Significant stenosis is present in the MCA
D: Normal velocity for the posterior cerebral artery
column2:
Answer: B. Significant stenosis is present in the basilar artery

Feedback:
The mean velocity demonstrates a significant increase at a depth of 105mm which is consistent with stenosis in the basilar
artery.

column1:
Which of the following is a symptom usually related to vertebrobasilar disease?

A: Dysphagia
B: Receptive aphasia
C: Expressive aphasia
D: Unilateral visual disturbances
column2:
Answer: A. Dysphagia

Feedback:
Dysphagia refers to difficulty swallowing which is controlled by the brain stem. The vertebrobasilar system supplies the brain
stem with blood. Unilateral visual disturbances, receptive aphasia, and expressive aphasia are symptoms related to ICA/MCA
disease.

column1:
Which of the following would lead to overestimation of stenosis in the internal carotid artery?

A: Significant aortic valve stenosis
B: Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side
C: Tandem CCA stenosis
D: Reduced systemic blood pressure
column2:
Answer: B. Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side

Feedback:
Overestimation of stenosis in the ICA can be seen with:
Systemic HTN
Compensatory flow due to stenosis on other side
Short area of focal stenosis
Smaller vessel size
Elevated heart rate
Measuring a compensatory heart beat with arrhythmias
Lower systemic BP, aortic valve stenosis and tandem CCA stenosis would all slow the flow into the ICA stenosis and reduce the
velocities in the ipsilateral ICA and underestimate ICA stenosis.

column1:
If you detect flow toward the transducer in the right ACA while using the temporal window,

A: there is probably an occlusion of the left ICA
B: there is probably an occlusion of the right ICA
C: there is probably a left MCA occlusion
D: the flow direction is normal
column2:
Answer: B. there is probably an occlusion of the right ICA

Feedback:
When evaluating the ACA flow from the transtemporal window, flow should be moving away from the transducer. If flow
direction is reversed in one of the anterior cerebral arteries, the change can be caused by cross-over collateralization. An ICA
occlusion should be suspected on the same side.

column1:
What is the minimum % area ICA stenosis that is considered hemodynamically significant and treatment is recommended?

A: 80%
B: 75%
C: 95%
D: 70%
column2:
Answer: D. 70%

Feedback:
A hemodynamically significant stenosis requiring intervention is defined as 70-99% blockage of the ICA.

column1:
If an acute left ICA occlusion is present, which of the following statements is true?

A: The right MCA flow will be decreased or absent with flow reversal in the right internal carotid siphon.
B: The right MCA will demonstrate decreased or absent flow.
C: The left ophthalmic artery will demonstrate decreased or absent flow
D: The right ophthalmic artery will demonstrate decreased or absent flow
column2:
Answer: C. The left ophthalmic artery will demonstrate decreased or absent flow

Feedback:
The ICA supplies the ipsilateral MCA and ophthalmic artery with blood. If the ICA is occluded the flow in these vessels will be
absent unless collateral vessels present which would lead to minimal flow in the vessels. Collaterals would not be present with
an acute occlusion.

column1:
Which of the following describes cerebral cross-over collateralization?

A: left ICA flow through posterior communicator to left vertebral
B: flow from the right MCA to the left MCA through a cross over collateral vessel
C: flow from the left ACA to the right ACA through the anterior communicating artery
D: flow from the left MCA to the right MCA through a cross over collateral vessel
column2:
Answer: C. flow from the left ACA to the right ACA through the anterior communicating artery

Feedback:
Cross over collateralization occurs when flow from one side of the brain crosses over midline to feed the opposite side. If an ICA
is occluded, flow moves from the contralateral ICA into the contralateral ACA and then through anterior communicator into the
ipsilateral ACA. An example would be: Right ICA occlusion = flow from the left ACA moves into the right ACA through the anterior
communicating artery. Flow toward the transducer in the ACA indicates ipsilateral ICA obstruction

column1:
Fibromuscular dysplasia most commonly affects which portion of the carotid arteries?

A: bulb and proximal ICA
B: mid segment of the ICA
C: CCA bifurcation
D: proximal ECA
column2:
Answer: B. mid segment of the ICA

Feedback:
FMD usually occurs in the mid or distal segment of the ICA, while atherosclerosis most commonly affects the proximal ICA.

column1:
Which of the following is most suggestive of a subclavian steal?

A: amaurosis fugax
B: systemic HTN
C: difference in brachial pressures >20 mmHg
D: congestive heart failure
column2:
Answer: C. difference in brachial pressures >20 mmHg

Feedback:
Amaurosis fugax is usually related to ICA stenosis. HTN is a non-specific symptom and not a complication of a subclavian steal.
Because the blood flow to the affected arm is being "pulled" from the brain, the pressure in the arm is much lower than the
unaffected side. BP levels in both arms that differ more than 15mmHg are highly suspicious for the presence of a subclavian
steal.

column1:
When using the suboccipital window for TCD evaluation with left lateral angulation, you detect low resistance flow moving toward the transducer with a mean flow velocity of 40cm/s at a depth of 60mm. These findings are most suggestive of:

A: abnormal left vertebral velocity and flow direction
B: normal left vertebral flow
C: normal right vertebral flow
D: normal left vertebral velocity with abnormal flow direction
column2:
Answer: D. normal left vertebral velocity with abnormal flow direction

Feedback:
The flow in the left vertebral is reversed but normal in velocity. A blockage in the proximal left subclavian artery can cause left
vertebral flow reversal as the distal left subclavian "steals" blood from the cerebral vessels.

column1:
Carotid body tumors are more common in:

A: men and people with Ehler-Danlos syndrome
B: men and people with Buerger disease
C: women and people living at sea level
D: women and people living at high altitudes
column2:
Answer: D. women and people living at high altitudes

Feedback:
CBT is more common in females and those living at higher altitudes. Higher elevations mean less oxygen in the air. Chronic
hypoxia leads to an overactive and enlarged CBT.

column1:
Wernicke aphasia is defined as:

A: Inability to swallow liquids
B: Inability to understand speech, speak, or follow directions
C: Inability to swallow solid foods
D: Inability to speak but can understand others
column2:
Answer: B. Inability to understand speech, speak, or follow directions

Feedback:
Wernicke aphasia (AKA receptive aphasia) is defined as the inability to understand speech, speak, or follow directions.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes Hollenhorst plaques?

A: Heavily calcified plaque that forms in the aortic arch
B: Most patients experience intermittent periods of unilateral blindness
C: Heavily calcified plaque that forms in the distal aorta
D: Usually seen on optometry exams
column2:
Answer: D. Usually seen on optometry exams

Feedback:
Hollenhorst plaques are cholesterol emboli that originate from atherosclerotic lesions in the ipsilateral carotid artery or aorta
and lodge in the small branches of the retinal artery. They are usually identified by an optometrist on a standard vision exam
because the patient is asymptomatic.

column1:
Which statement best describes the findings on the image?

A: Neointimal hyperplasia has developed around a stent.
B: There is soft atheroma formation within the CCA.
C: There is heterogeneous, complex atheroma formation within the CCA.
D: The image demonstrates and area of recanalization through a thrombus.
column2:
Answer: B. There is soft atheroma formation within the CCA.

Feedback:
The image demonstrates homogeneous, soft atheroma formation within the CCA lumen.

column1:
What statement describes a TIA?

A: Stroke symptoms lasting 2-4 weeks
B: Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs or less
C: Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs - 2 weeks
D: Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs - 72 hrs
column2:
Answer: B. Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs or less

Feedback:
TIA = Stroke symptoms lasting 24 hrs or less
CVA = Stroke symptoms lasting more than 24hrs

column1:
A patient presents for a carotid ultrasound with a history of myocardial infarction and a left ventricular assist device is present. Which of the following describes the expected flow pattern in the normal common carotid artery?

A: high-velocity flow with accelerated upstroke
B: triphasic waveform with increased systolic velocity
C: low-velocity flow with delayed upstroke and loss of pulsatility
D: antegrade flow in systole and retrograde flow in diastole
column2:
Answer: C. low-velocity flow with delayed upstroke and loss of pulsatility

Feedback:
Left Ventricular Assist Device (LVAD) causes CCA Doppler waveforms with low-velocity flow, delayed upstroke and loss of
pulsatility. Carotid Doppler waveform morphology is highly variable among patients with LVADs and is very dependent on pump
settings. It is very important to document the presence of a left ventricular assist device or intra-aortic balloon pump on the
preliminary report. The waveform contour cannot be evaluated in these patients.

column1:
